arc_summary: The introduction is built on a persistent **interlacing of M1 territory and M2 gap** sentences, cycling through four distinct limitation zones (static datasets, RL convergence, self-generated data degradation, exploration-exploitation trade-off) before committing to M3. **Two M3 announcements** anchor the argument: an early thesis at sentence 15 and a fuller construct-naming block from sentences 27–32. The paper's central move is reframing antagonism as synergy — **exploration-exploitation becomes 'compounding innovation'** — before a brief M2 return (sentences 33, 35) sharpens the remaining gap and cues the final structural announcement.

move_roles.role: Establishes the scaling thesis as the foundational principle of modern AI, anchoring the entire introduction in a widely accepted empirical lesson.
Identifies the structural limitation of static, exogenous datasets and asserts the necessity of self-generated data, opening the first gap the paper will address.
Introduces reinforcement learning as the existing solution to self-generated data, building up the expectation of dramatic RL results to set up an immediate contrast.
Deflates the RL expectation with a single contrastive pivot, signalling that the anticipated scaling gains do not materialise.
Documents two concrete failure modes (RL convergence and NLP self-training) to substantiate the gap, while also surveying the current wave of self-generated data research.
Pins down a specific degradation mechanism in self-generated NLP training, sharpening the gap immediately before the first thesis announcement.
Delivers a first, compressed thesis announcement — multi-scale, multi-agent self-organisation as the path beyond the data-degradation constraint — planting the paper's flag early before cycling back.
Resumes territory review by characterising when RL amplification works well, establishing the boundary condition that makes the next gap concrete.
Introduces the large-state-space failure mode — novel behaviours cannot be discovered by chance — and mechanistically unpacks why no learning gradient is available.
Surveys the landscape of existing solutions (adaptive environments, intrinsic motivation, curiosity models) to show the field's best responses to the exploration problem, setting up the residual gap.
Identifies the pernicious residual problem that all prior approaches leave unsolved: the direction of exploration still requires external designer decisions.

Names and defines the paper's central construct — compounding innovation — situates it in biological evolution and human cultural history, and reframes exploration-exploitation from trade-off to synergy.
Poses the rhetorical question of whether biological compounding innovation can be replicated artificially, briefly reopening the gap to motivate the paper's empirical and design programme.
Advances multi-agent systems as a candidate path to ongoing learning, pivoting back toward the paper's positive contribution.
Concedes that current multi-agent algorithms still fail at compounding innovation, preserving the niche just before the closing structural announcement.
Announces the paper's organisational plan — three biologically-grounded social structures — and previews the first (collective living), signalling how the argument will unfold.

sentences.text: A key lesson in artificial intelligence (AI) history is that the extent to which intelligent behaviour emerges in learning systems scales with the size of the dataset used for training.
Current large models are trained on vast datasets and obtain human-level performance on a wide variety of tasks, especially in natural language, but increasingly in multi-modal domains.
Though large, these training datasets are exogenous to the models they train; consequently, their learning is dependent upon, and limited to, relationships in the data.
sentences.note: Introduces a structural limitation of the current paradigm — datasets are static and external.
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: contrast sentence immediately follows territory-establishing sentences

sentences.text: There may be limits to what can be achieved by training on any such static dataset, even if it is very large.
sentences.note: Asserts the significance of a fundamental limitation; establishes it as real and worth attending to.

sentences.text: For example, an algorithm trained on all of human knowledge before the performance of the double-slit experiment which catalysed the development of quantum physics would have no way of predicting the outcome in advance.
sentences.note: Concrete illustration of the limitation claimed in [4], which is a primary (emph) parent.

sentences.text: To overcome this, algorithms need to generate their own data.
sentences.note: Asserts a necessary condition for progress; establishes its importance as motivation for the paper's direction.

sentences.text: Reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms do this.
sentences.note: Reports an established property of RL systems.
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: returns to territory after gap framing

sentences.text: They learn by interacting with their environment and observing new states reached through their own behaviour, in effect generating continually-growing datasets for themselves.
sentences.note: Defines how RL achieves self-generated data, unpacking [7] (tempstat parent). Legal chain.

sentences.text: RL agents running in simulation can generate data indefinitely, so if their cognitive abilities scale with dataset size, you would expect dramatic results from this approach.
sentences.note: Asserts the significance and expected power of RL's data-generation capacity; functions as a primary claim establishing why this approach matters. Corrected from extension:speculative — [8] was a secondary parent, which is illegal.

sentences.text: However, this is not what typically happens.
sentences.note: Introduces tension against the expectation set up in [9].
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: counter-claim against prior territory sentence

sentences.text: In small and stationary worlds, RL agents converge to behaviours that repeatedly execute the same actions in the same states, generating no new data.
sentences.note: Reports an established finding about RL convergence behavior.
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: backs up the contrast in [10] with a documented finding

sentences.text: When this happens their learning stagnates.
sentences.note: States the consequence of the convergence described in [11] (tempstat parent). Legal chain.

sentences.text: In natural language processing, a wave of research is seeing performance improvement from models generating their own training data in sophisticated ways.
sentences.note: Reports a current trend in the NLP literature.

sentences.text: However, naively training a model on data generated by itself tends to remove the tails of the distribution, degrading the data stream produced and thus limiting subsequent learning.
sentences.note: Introduces a limitation/failure mode of the self-generated data approach described in [13].
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: gap identified immediately after territory claim

sentences.text: In this article, we argue that next-level intelligent systems may move beyond this constraint by continually generating novel data in self-organising multi-scale, multi-agent interactions.
sentences.note: Announces the thesis and purpose of the current paper.
sentences.move_note: First M3 announcement; followed by cycling back to M1/M2

sentences.text: RL algorithms strengthen rewarding actions even if they are rarely emitted, effectively amplifying the most profitable available behaviour.
sentences.note: Reports an established property of RL algorithms.
sentences.move_note: M2→M3 cycling: returns to territory after first M3 announcement

sentences.text: This works well if the initial behaviour distribution covers all relevant states.
sentences.note: States a known boundary condition of RL — an established technical fact about when the approach succeeds, not speculation. Corrected from extension:speculative.

sentences.text: However, when the world is large enough that the initial behaviour distribution covers only a relatively tiny subspace, an intelligent agent must invent wholly new behaviours which they are extraordinarily unlikely to produce by accident.
sentences.note: Introduces the limitation that applies when the state space is large, contrasting with [17].
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: gap contrasting with prior territory sentence

sentences.text: The agent's data stream may change through its actions, but the waiting time until a complex rewarding behaviour is emitted by chance may be so long that no learning gradient is available, thus such changes do not motivate further exploration.
sentences.note: Elaborates mechanistically on the limitation described in [18] (contrast parent). Legal chain.

sentences.text: One way to improve exploration in RL algorithms is to have the environment adapt to the agent.
sentences.note: Reports an established approach in the literature.
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: reviews prior work addressing the gap

sentences.text: Another is to make exploration an explicit part of the goal.
sentences.note: Reports another established approach; parallel to [20].

sentences.text: In intrinsic motivation models, the latter is achieved by incorporating models of curiosity that seek novel states or via agents that generate their own intrinsic goals.
sentences.note: Reports specific implementations of the approach mentioned in [21].

sentences.text: However, this leads to a trade-off: computation must be balanced between seeking new goals (exploration), and improving behaviours on existing goals (exploitation).
sentences.note: Introduces a limitation of the intrinsic motivation approach — the exploration-exploitation trade-off.
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: gap identified against prior territory

sentences.text: Substantial research effort has been devoted to balancing this trade-off, and some bounds are known.
sentences.note: Summarizes the state of prior research on the exploration-exploitation trade-off.
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: territory review of prior work on the gap

sentences.text: Some approaches attempt to evade this trade-off, for example by focusing on the intrinsic goals where they make most progress.
sentences.note: Reports an existing class of approaches that try to sidestep the trade-off.

sentences.text: But a pernicious difficulty remains: even if the designer can avoid the exploration-exploitation trade-off, they must still decide the direction of exploration.
sentences.note: Identifies a residual problem not solved by existing approaches; a gap/limitation claim against [25].
sentences.move_note: Interlaced M1/M2: residual gap after survey of prior work

sentences.text: By contrast, in the view we develop here, exploration and exploitation become synergistic rather than antagonistic.
sentences.note: Introduces the paper's alternative framework in contrast to the prior literature.
sentences.move_note: Second M3 block following M2→M3 cycling

sentences.text: Exploitation drives exploration in the appropriate direction as innovation builds upon prior innovation.
sentences.note: Unpacks the synergistic relationship claimed in [27] (contribution parent). Legal chain.

sentences.text: We call this effect compounding innovation.
sentences.note: Introduces and names the central theoretical construct the paper will use.

sentences.text: Mechanistically, compounding innovation occurs when exploitation continuously generates new data that creates opportunities for learning: exploration by exploitation.
sentences.note: Defines the mechanism underlying the construct named in [29] (framedef parent). Legal chain.

sentences.text: We argue compounding innovation arises in biological evolution because the environment continually changes with innovation due to specific types of interactions between biological units.
sentences.note: States a core argument of the paper about the biological basis of compounding innovation.

sentences.text: These interactions gave rise to human cultural evolution—the process that generated the data on which our most intelligent algorithms have trained.
sentences.note: Makes a cross-domain claim linking biological interactions to cultural evolution and AI training data; treated as tempstat per Rule 3 (cross-domain generalization).

sentences.text: Can we create analogous interactions in artificial learning systems?
sentences.note: Corrected from new_tag_needed. The rhetorical question identifies an open, unresolved problem the paper is motivated to address — the core function of nostat. Rhetorical question form does not exclude the tag.
sentences.move_note: Brief return to M2 gap-framing via rhetorical question after M3 claims; part of M2→M3 cycling

sentences.text: In a multi-agent system, the behaviour of any one agent influences the data streams of others, suggesting a possible path to ongoing learning.
sentences.note: Asserts the significance and relevance of multi-agent systems as a potential solution; establishes a phenomenon worth attending to.

sentences.text: Yet contemporary multi-agent algorithms still fail at compounding innovation.
sentences.note: Introduces a gap/limitation — current multi-agent approaches do not yet achieve compounding innovation.
sentences.move_note: Final M2 gap before closing M3 structural announcement

sentences.text: Here, we consider three broad forms of social structure that arise in biological systems, each characterised by distinct types of social interaction that may contribute to compounding innovation in different ways.
sentences.note: Announces the organizational scope and structure of the paper.
sentences.move_note: Final M3 block; announces paper structure

sentences.text: First, we discuss the implications of 'collective living': how competition between agents creates novel data by disrupting local equilibria, often generating naturally emergent sequences of learning opportunities that stimulate behavioural innovation.
sentences.note: Announces the first major topic/section and its argument; part of the paper's structural plan introduced in [36].
